name: Vietnamese

formality.description: Vietnamese formality is expressed almost entirely through pronoun choice. There is no verb conjugation for formality. The pronoun system encodes age, gender, and social relationship: anh/chị (older brother/sister, common polite), em (younger person), bạn (friend/peer, neutral), quý vị (distinguished, very formal), tôi (I, neutral formal). Software UI typically uses bạn (neutral, peer-like) for second person, which is friendly without being presumptuous.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Vietnamese pronouns encode perceived age and gender relative to the speaker (anh = older male, chị = older female). Software UI should use bạn (gender-neutral peer) to avoid assumptions about user age and gender.

neutral-ban.label: Bạn (Neutral)
neutral-ban.description: Standard for software UI. Uses bạn (neutral peer) for second person. Friendly and appropriate for most digital products.
neutral-ban.prompt: Professional Vietnamese. Use bạn for second person (neutral, peer-level — appropriate for software UI). Use tôi for first person when needed. Clear, modern Vietnamese appropriate for digital interfaces. Avoid overly formal constructions.

formal-quyvi.label: Quý vị (Formal)
formal-quyvi.description: For government, banking, legal, or contexts addressing a general public audience with respect.
formal-quyvi.prompt: Formal Vietnamese. Use quý vị for second-person plural/respectful address, quý khách for customer-facing contexts. Elevated register appropriate for government, banking, and official communications.

notes: Previous default had no pronoun guidance. Added explicit bạn/quý vị pronoun choice which is critical for appropriate Vietnamese translation.
